Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have bursts where I'd watch a ton of lecture videos in one sitting, then go days without touching anything. I know I should have been more consistent, but I always felt like I could catch up later since I'm pretty good at cramming and understanding material quickly. Most of my actual studying happened late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was going on in my life and when deadlines were looming. I rarely had set study times because my schedule with extracurriculars and work was unpredictable. Sometimes I'd go two weeks barely logging in, then suddenly spend an entire weekend trying to absorb everything I'd missed. It wasn't ideal, but somehow I managed to do well on exams.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline or realizing I had no idea what was happening in the course. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a discussion post or new material and that would remind me to check in. Fear was honestly my biggest motivator - that scared feeling that I was falling behind would finally push me to log in and see what I'd missed.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a habit for me - it was always something I used reactively rather than proactively. I tried a few times to make it part of my morning routine, but that never stuck. The only time it felt somewhat routine was during exam periods when I was constantly checking for review materials or practice questions.
Q5: The lecture videos were genuinely helpful because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewind when concepts didn't click immediately. I also found the downloadable slides really useful because I could annotate them and study them offline. The discussion boards occasionally had good insights from other students that helped me see things from different angles.
Q6: I basically ignored the peer interaction features and a lot of the optional readings. With limited resources, I couldn't afford to spend time on things that weren't directly going to help my grade. The supplementary materials felt like nice-to-haves rather than necessities, so they were the first things I cut when I was short on time, which was always.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on the exam or in assignments. I needed to see a clear payoff for the effort I was putting in. If something seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly going to improve my understanding of testable material, I just couldn't justify spending my limited energy on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to, even if I was rushing through them at the last minute. With optional stuff, my anxiety would actually spike because I'd feel guilty about not doing them, but then I'd rationalize that my time was better spent on what was mandatory. It was this constant internal battle.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download all the PDFs and lecture slides at the beginning of the semester when I had good wifi access, then study from those on my laptop without internet. I also used my own notes extensively and sometimes found YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials.
Q10: There were whole weeks where I was deeply engaged with the course content but never logged into the LMS. I'd be reviewing my downloaded notes on the bus, doing practice problems from a textbook I borrowed from the library, or working through concepts with a study partner. None of that would show up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, then download the slides and never return to the video. I'd screenshot important diagrams and study those on my phone during breaks at my part-time job. My actual engagement with materials extended way beyond that initial access point that the LMS would record.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, I was scared I was falling behind in everything, and I just couldn't bring myself to face the LMS because seeing all the incomplete tasks made me feel worse. The mindfulness exercises helped somewhat, but there were definitely weeks where I was paralyzed by fear and just avoided everything.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed with other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing to go. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but 'later' kept getting pushed back. The fatigue made it hard to concentrate even when I did log in, so sometimes I'd just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely - worrying about money, thinking about other deadlines, just zoning out. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them just to feel like I was doing something. It was engagement theater more than actual learning.
Q15: I avoided assignments constantly even though I knew they mattered. The fear of not doing them well enough would paralyze me, so I'd procrastinate until the anxiety of the deadline outweighed the anxiety of potentially failing. It's a terrible cycle - I know the work is important, but the scared feeling makes me avoid it until I absolutely can't anymore.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the only thing that could cut through my avoidance and force me to actually engage. My LMS usage probably spiked dramatically in the 24-48 hours before anything was due. Without deadlines, I honestly don't know if I would have engaged with the course at all - they were my external structure.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. Good feedback motivated me to engage more, but critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that course for a while. I appreciated knowing where I stood, but sometimes I'd delay looking at feedback because I was scared of what it might say.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace when life got hectic, bad because without rigid structure, I'd drift and disengage. The 18 online courses I was juggling made it hard to give any single course consistent attention.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier given my limited internet situation. But the sheer volume of content was overwhelming - it made it hard to know what to prioritize. Clearer guidance on what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me manage my anxiety about missing things.
Q20: I think that could be students like me who are anxious and checking things obsessively without actually completing work. Or people who are trying to engage but getting stuck or confused. High activity doesn't mean productive activity - sometimes you're just spinning your wheels or using the LMS as a form of procrastination from actual assignments.
Q21: That's literally me. I barely showed up in the LMS but I got a distinction because I'm good at understanding material quickly and performing on exams. I did most of my real studying offline with downloaded materials. The LMS activity is just one narrow window into learning - it doesn't capture what happens in your head or outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, but they can't show comprehension or real engagement. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing that happens away from the screen, the discussions with classmates in person. They definitely can't capture whether someone was actually focused or just had a tab open while doing something else.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and thinking deeply about the material, not just going through the motions. It's when concepts click and you find yourself thinking about them even outside of study time. For me, engagement is mental investment - which doesn't always correlate with how often I log into a platform or complete tasks on time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my constant anxiety about the course, the hours I spent studying from downloaded materials, or the genuine interest I developed in some topics despite my poor attendance record. It wouldn't show that I was scared but still trying, or that my low completion rates didn't mean I wasn't learning. My exam scores tell a different story than my engagement metrics, and I think that matters.
